title: 2025/08/25 - EW Subcommittee B [Part 2]
displayTitle: 2025-08-25 - Education and Workforce Committee - Employment Relations Amendment Bill [Subcommittee B, Part 2]
description: Monday, 25 August 2025 | Education and Workforce subcommittee B Employment Relations Amendment Bill Submitters: Community Law Centres Aotearoa - Rupert O’Brien, Law Reform Coordinator - Lars Hansen - Toby Cooper Ruth Chapman Mary Bowden Uber - Pia Brunner, Uber's Director Public Policy, Australia and New Zealand - Cameron Loughlin, Uber's Director Legal, Australia and New Zealand RCSA - Brooke Lord, Head of Advocacy FairPlay Legal - Megan Gundesen, Director Freightways Group - Mark Troughear, Chief Executive - Ami Van Gils, Head of People and Culture
descriptionSummary: Employment Relations Amendment Bill. Community Law Centres Aotearoa. Rupert O’Brien, Law Reform Coordinator

chunks.text: Comar, were charitable trust.
Do we have, um,
Tim, Tim, do we have them coming in? Okay. Um, do we have Ruth Chapman available, or who is that? The, or Mary Bowden if community law do we community law centers. Uh, if we start with them,

we have the community law. Great. Thank you. Um, KDA Kilda. Hi, um, Rupert, we can see your, your name come up. Here we go. Thank you very much. Um, we have 10 minutes with you this morning. Um, you can use that time as you wish. If you want to, um, leave any time for, um, for any questions, go ahead.

Um, yeah, we, I, we've called a bit early, I think were we on at 1214? Yeah, we're just, yeah. Oh, we go technical with other things. Yeah. Okay, cool. I've got a couple of colleagues who are supposed to be joining me, uh, as well to assist with this submission. So I just, I can see Lars has managed to join. I just give, I wonder if we could make a few minutes for us to hopefully have Toby join as well, if that's okay. Sorry, Just, um, if you can get started, are you able to get started without them or not? Yep. We,

Um, Lars and I can get started and hopefully Toby will be able to join in as we go. Great. All right. Um, so Koto, my name is Rut O'Brien. I am one of the law reform coordinators for community law centers, roa. Um, and I'm joined by my colleague Lar Hanson, who is an experienced employment, uh, solicitor from the community law center in Taranaki.

Uh, hopefully we'll be joined by Toby Cooper as well, from Disability Law, which is our specialist Law Center for Disabled People and their rights. Um, community law provides free legal advice to those who can't afford it and can't otherwise access legal assistance or representation. We are often assisting people who are in small employment, uh, small employers, those who aren't unionized, um, and people on casual or contractor arrangements.

We are have 24 community law centers across the country, and we, employment is our biggest regularly either the biggest or second biggest, uh, area of law that we advise on. So we have a lot of experience in this area. Um, and this submission is from law centers, which is the peak body for all 24 community law centers.

So the submission was written in, uh, consultation with all 24 law centers and represents our, our views as a whole. Um, I'll pass to Lars to discuss our issues with the contractor, uh, area in particular, and then Toby will hopefully be along to discuss the, uh, our, our issues with the compensation rules, the changes to section 1, 2, 3 being the primary issues that we wanted to talk about today.

Um, Kilda la Thank you Ru, and good afternoon. Committee members. Um, my oral submissions focus on the contractor proposal in three main areas, sham contracts, exploitation of vulnerable and uncertainty and litigation. In regards to 0.1, the sham contracts, the proposal promotes form over substance in the contractual relationship and creates a gateway attest that is controlled by one party, the offerer.

As long as the written form complies, there is no ability to challenge the contract's employment. This proposal removes the current safeguards against abuse of form, which is well highlighted by the employment court when they look under Section six. Looking at the real nature of the employment relationship or the relationship in regard to 0.2 exploitation.

There are some people that do not and will not have the business acumen to be contractors. This may present in various aspects of the person's vulnerability. Financial, economic, age, financial circumstances, uh, sorry, family circumstances or intellectual ability. These are the people that we see, and we have seen examples of the use of the contractor agreement when they're truly employees.

The sole purpose of contractor agreements for these individuals was and is to remove their basic employment rights. This proposed change would place these people at a clear disadvantage, especially when the power is held by the offerer of the working relationship. This is the inherent power imbalance recognized under section three of the Employment Relations Act.

Simply put, vulnerable and desperate people will accept whatever they can get in regards to uncertainty and litigation. The proposal has a high potential of shifting litigation from the employment regime to the civil regime, and also potentially increasing the amount of litigation.

The main points there would be contractual undue influence contracts for minors. Uh, just as a passing comment, we see contracts for children coming through our offices, unconscionable bargaining and just unconscionable contracts in its whole.

Now, um, that was the substance of my oral submissions, and we were going to move to Toby from this point. Um, I see he's not in yet. Rupert, is there anything you'd like to add? Yeah, Klar, I'll, um, quickly just cover our concerns regarding the changes to, uh, section 1, 2, 3, and 1, 2, 4 being those, uh, relating to contribution.

Um, we have really serious concerns with these changes. They look to alter the current employment system by HU in a huge way. Uh, it's not really reflected in the explanatory note or the, um, intent of this bill. The level to which these changes might change the employment landscape.

Um, in particular, we have serious concerns about the removal of contribution, uh, like the removal of compensation, whether there has been any employee contribution to the, uh, personal grievance arising. What we see is that there is almost always some relationship breakdown. It often starts from the employer's side, but the employee may react before they get advice, and they may be considered to have contributed in some small way.

But what this change proposes to do is completely remove all compensation for any, uh, contribution, uh, and with any, without any kind of level of proportionality. So where you have an employee or who has treated an employee very poorly, the employee may lose complete rights to any compensation with being the primary reason, uh, primary thing that we apply for, to account for, uh, breaches of good faith and of natural justice for an employee.

They remove all ability to get that. If they have some sort of error on their behavior, some small level of contribution, they will lose that, right? Um, the ERA already has a well-functioning system to deal with. Contribution commonly, uh, employee compensation is reduced even up to a hundred percent.

It does happen whether the contribution is serious, it's already well dealt with by the ERA, and their flexibility should remain. We also have a real serious problem with the serious misconduct, uh, issue, uh, contribution, where the employer is given free reign to completely breach, um, natural justice, where they believe that the employee has what has committed, what they believe is serious misconduct.

Um, serious misconduct isn't defined in law. It can be defined in the contract as well. And the proposed change is that where an employee has committed serious misconduct, all their rights are out the window. The employer may procedurally follow, no, you may follow no legitimate procedure. They may dismiss the employee without telling them why.

They may not give them any right to respond. Uh, they could embarrass them or, or yell at them in front of their colleagues, uh, and the employee would have absolutely no ability to get any kind of comeback for that kind of issue. And so the employer, and even the employer could bring up a serious misconduct issue months or even years after it's happened.

And the way that this law is written currently is that even at that point, the employee would have no rights. Um, I'll leave rooms for some questions now, hopefully. Oh, Toby, did you want to add anything? I see you've joined. Hi, Rupert. Uh, no, I think you, you've said it mostly quite well. I did. Um, I'm not sure if you had said, um, but look, as an alternative suggestion, if the government seeks to restrict access to remedies, this should cut both ways.

So, if the government is genuinely committed to both a balance in judicial outcomes and setting barriers on access to remedies, this should be imposed on both employers and employees alike. So, claims by employers such as restraint of trade claims or breach of confidentiality claims should also be subject to the same barriers.

So this would not be our preferred approach as it would not address our concerns about the failure to deliver proportional remedies. However, it would maintain a greater degree of balance in the system than the current proposals alone. I think apart from that group, that you've, uh, summed up our position quite well.

Thanks. Wonderful. Thank you so much for your, um, submission. We don't have any questions at this stage, so appreciate you taking the time to, um, to present to us today. Sure. Thank you. Thank you. Uh, it looks as though we have Ruth Chapman, um, coming on.

I'm sorry that I'm looking like a sword. Oh, Well, yeah. Oh, I turned my video on. That might help. Well, That, that'll help. Otherwise, we're expecting some sharp comments from you, Ruth. Um, so, um, you've got, uh, five minutes with us, um, and you can use that time to include questions if you wish. Thank you. Uh, so I've just been listening to the community law one, and I agree with, um, the comments that they have made.

I think that at base, the bill talks about re tilting with an intention to, um, move, um, more protections to employers on the assumption that they don't have enough. Uh, I think that the bill actually goes the other way, that it, it tilts, um, protections under the act, uh, further away from employees and, and in fact find much of the language.

Quite ironic, really, um, in that sense, I did say in the introduction, uh, to my brief submission that I used to work in a role where people would often come to see me because they'd suddenly discovered at work that somebody was criticizing their work. And, um, and many of them didn't know what to do next, and particularly if they did not belong to, um, a union.

So, uh, the, the issues around the, um, inability to take personal grievances for employees on higher wages, uh, those people would alre already under the old law feel really, um, isolated. And I did recall while I was writing this, that, um, so we need to go back something like 30 years probably, uh, to the time when, uh, people on individual contracts, this was under the ECA, uh, were not a, were not able to take grievances.

My my sister who was in a job, which these days I'm sure would be over the $180,000, was, I understand the first person in New Zealand to take a personal grievance against her employer for wrongful dismissal. Uh, and she won. Um, so I'm really puzzled about this.

I just, the ability to give an employer the right to dismiss somebody for, um, no, and to not be required to say why and not give the employer the chance to challenge. It just seems a complete abuse of natural justice, um, to me. And so I, well, words fail me, as I said, every time I, every time I go back and look at the bill, as I was preparing for this, I just found myself, um, boiling really with rage ab about that to, to often in, in all levels of employment,

particularly in senior levels. Um, as in my sister's case, uh, the, her immediate boss took a dislike to her, and that was enough for him to make it possible for her to have to leave. Um, I also wanna pick up on one of the community law things, which was that when things go wrong, it's really easy for people to do things which are not by the book not desirable.

What this bill does is it says that it's okay for employers to do that. So in other words, um, in remedies around personal grievances, if they've done some small things wrong, that shouldn't be taken into account. But if employees have done some things wrong, that should be taken into account and prevent them from getting the remedies that they are, are seeking fear, um, isolation, uh, feelings of being bullied, all sorts of things, whether right or wrong, often lead to, uh, some issues, um, like that.

Um, I won't talk about the, um, serious misconduct 'cause I think other people have covered with that. I'll just conclude by saying I believe that in a number of ways around the removal of the 30 day rule, uh, all of the aspects that what this bill does is increase the precariousness of employment for, uh, employees in New Zealand.

Uh, and it, it is already a difficult thing for people, but to put n near so many more people at risk in their employment, I think is unconscionable. Great. Um, we've actually met time, so thank you very much for your submission today, Ruth, really appreciate you coming and presenting to us.
Thank you. Cheers. Um, and next we have Mary Bowden. Um, it looks like we've got Mary on, um, on the phone. Um, Mary, so can you hear us, Mary, able to speak?
Hopefully Mary is able to speak. Can you unmute yourself, Mary, and if possible, if you've got camera access on your phone, that'd be great too.

Okay. So, um, doesn't look like we are getting Mary through, so if we could, uh, Mary, are you there? Yes. Can you hear me? Yes, we can. Alright, Mary, thank you for your time today. Um, we have, um, five minutes with you if you want to start, that's great, including time for questions.

Okay. Well, well, thank you for the opportunity. Um, this is my personal submission and I'm taking a, a, um, I appreciate the previous speakers have really gotten into the weeds, and I'm taking a bit more of a, a wider view. Um, and for, for context, I mean, I thought in all of this, um, you know, those who've gone before us, having fought two world wars and suffered through deprivation, sought a better life for them and their dependence.

And this lead to New Zealand adopting an egalitarian way of life. And we have to ensure that legacy and continues down the generations. Part of that legacy is that New Zealand is a safe, secure, civil, relatively sane society. So especially for overseas businesses, it is, in my opinion, a privilege to operate here.

It's not a right and, and there's a price to pay for operating here. You pay taxes like GST income tax, tax on profits. You don't offshore your profits. You do it on our terms and conditions. You respect and abide by our laws, you respect our people and the dignity of our people.

Otherwise, such a business is expecting New Zealand and New Zealanders to subsidize their activity. And we got rid of subsidies in the eighties. And I speak here of subsidies in the wider term, not just in terms of the direct dollar cost, but in terms of those other things that previous speakers such Ruth as Ruth Chapman talked to, where you change the terms and conditions so that the, the level playing field is tilted in, in one party's favor.

So business is free to operate within their terms and conditions, and if not, they're free to choose to adapt their business model to their terms and conditions, or if they choose not to, they're free to go elsewhere. If the New Zealand, uh, uh, and I'm speaking here regarding taxis.

If New Zealand a New Zealand owned taxi business does not operate by the current terms and conditions paying its employees correctly, it will be alphas and it may fail and it's in direct sanctioned. This is how bad actors are restrained in a market economy.

We don't subsidize bad actors or people trying to bypass the terms and conditions. So in that respect, overseas business is subject to the same market forces as local business. They are not immune the level we want a level field playing field, not a tilted one.

Now, our business environment, we have minimum wage, and the minimum wage in New Zealand was praised by none other than Mark Twain when he was here. We have health and safety laws, which, which structure work breaks and will rest breaks to protect workers and customers, but employees as independent contractors, as per my understanding of the Uber business model, allows a platform taxi service to apparently bypass our rules which protect our citizens, visitors and workers.

Our employment authority ruled the Uber drivers were not able to generate goodwill value to their business via the app. As a result, there would be no owner's equity from a loyal customer base, which could be onsold to another business owner. Say, if someone wants to retire, the Uber model appears to prevent this and controls which driver gets which customer for whatever fear.

And the driver appears to me to be largely kept into the dark at the beginning of the fair, whether it's a five or a 10 or a $50 or a hundred dollars fee. Thank you, Mary. Um, we have time with you. So, And I just to conclude with a, with what a, a Queenstown taxi driver said to me, I asked him what he thought of Uber and he said Uber is, is a charity and that driver donate their time to it.

And I'll leave you thanks. And I thank you for your time. Thank you, Mary. Appreciate it. Um, and uh, so next up we have Uber, uh, we've got Pierre Brenner and, um, Cameron Lachlan from, um, Uber. Uh, thank you very much. Um, we have 10 minutes with you, uh, to use as you wish with time for questions if you so desire.

Thank you Chair, uh, Koto, our members of the Select committee. Uh, my name is Cameron Lachlan and I'm here with my colleague, uh, Pierre Bruner on behalf of Uber to discuss our submission. We appreciate this opportunity to speak with the Select committee about this critically important bill. Since launching over a decade ago, Uber has become an integral part of daily life for nearly one in two Kiwis, connecting them to work friends Hanau and their favorite local businesses.

Last year alone, Uber and Uber Eats generated 1.5 billion direct and indirect economic value for communities across our tier, as well as opportunities for tens of thousands of people to earn and work flexibly in a way that fits around sporting family health commitments or other work. In our submission, we have shared feedback on some of our drivers and delivery people.

They're just a few of the more than 40,000 Kiwis who choose to earn with us. 91% of our earners say that they wouldn't continue if this flexibility were removed. The Bill's intent to clarify contractor status is a crucial step towards providing certainty for these individuals and the businesses that engage them.

While we strongly support the intent of the bill, we believe three vital amendments are necessary to ensure the Bill's intent is met. First, the proposed gateway test needs to cover businesses that operate on a facilitation model, not just a direct engagement model. This is critical for businesses like Uber where drivers perform work facilitated by our technology.

The facilitation model is recognized in the Land Transport Act and under the act, small passenger service operators include facilitators which are defined as businesses that connect drivers and passengers. By electronic means. Uber is such a facilitator, we propose clarification to the bill because it's currently drafted.

Facilitation businesses, including ours, will be left out. Second, we urge the committee to support the application of the contractor criteria to existing contracts with an appropriate savings provision for disputes filed before the bills introduction into parliament. Without it, businesses will be vulnerable to reclassification ca claims going back six years, applying the current uncertain law and creating an uneven playing field for new entrants to the market.

The government has the ability to make any law apply retrospectively where it's required to provide clarification or certainty. This is one of the rare situations envisaged by the legislation design and Advisory Committee guidelines where backwards looking legal clarification is appropriate. This will ensure the bill delivers on the government's objective of providing certainty and prevent extensive resource intensive litigation that could burden both businesses and the legal system.

Finally, to simplify assessment and reduce further disputes, we propose an amendment to clarify that if a contract provides a worker with the substantive right set out in the gateway test, the test is met. These aren't just technical suggestions. Uh, they're crucial to safeguarding the flexible earning opportunities that many, uh, thousands of New Zealanders rely on every day.
We are committed to working constructively with this committee to ensure the bill achieves its intended outcome. Uh, thank you and we welcome. Any questions?

Um, I don't see any questions. Oh, sorry. Oui. Go ahead. Um, thank you Madam Chair. Not so much a question. Um, I I, I guess just a reaction from my side in terms of the argument for retrospective. My sense is that certainty requires certainty of the law in a, in a forward going way.

Um, and I'm just, I'm not convinced that the arguments are made to impose retrospective legislation at all. Um, I think it's, it's there in terms of the argument is really one, in terms of litigation costs as opposed to certainty of the forward going law. Um, I'm just wondering if you have a, a reaction to that.

Yeah, it's a legitimate question. Uh, where should the certainty start? And we heard deliver easy, uh, speak in the other committee this morning, uh, about their concern where one of their workers, uh, might bring a claim and had they been engaged for 12 months prior to the introduction of the bill in 12 months subsequent they would have two different legal statuses, which in itself shows that the complexity that we're dealing with here, uh, to achieve, uh, true certainty and consistency, uh, Uber's view as the test should apply

to any claim moving forward. Uh, but we accept that any claim that, uh, is filed at the date of the bill, uh, could be assessed using the existing law. Um, if, if that's not the position, uh, there's, there's sort of a six year lookback, uh, period and it's clear, uh, to Uber and of, and from some of the other submissions that we've seen from taxi companies and postal services companies, uh, that, that businesses could be looking, um, at, at existing, uh, liability because they've relied on the arrangements for the last six years.

Okay. Well, I don't think we have any further questions for you. Um, appreciate you taking your time to, um, spend with the committee this, um, this afternoon. Um, thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you.

Okay. Um, next we have RCSA. Um, and so we have, um, 10 minutes with you and, um, welcome Brooke. Um, thank you for your time. So you can use that 10 minutes as you wish with time for questions if you want. Tremendous Kiara, everybody, and thank you so much for the opportunity to, um, speak with you today.

We, um, at RCSA are the peak industry body for the recruitment and staffing sector, um, in, in New Zealand and in Australia. And, um, that would be labor hire, it would be permanent placement recruitment, um, and all of our members across both countries, um, engage independent contractors, um, oftentimes and supply them to clients.

One of the challenges we are gonna come at this bill with is, um, a slightly different and bespoke concern around the gateway contact, the contact a gateway test. We respect the test and we think it's fantastic that the government we welcome moves to try and clarify and give certainty to employers around setting a test. The concern that we have with it is that it, it has had a quite a narrow focus on a particular segment of the contracting marketplace and perhaps hasn't looked to some of the unintended consequences that can flow, um,

to other forms of contracting in the marketplace from its introduction. So, um, RCSA and its members are strongly aligned with government, um, government's intent here around allowing workers to choose contracting if they so wish. And we do know from work here in Australia and New Zealand that the vast bulk of people who are working, um, for platform-based work and food delivery, often choosing that way to, to work that way.

They want to be contractors, they want to run multiple platforms at once. They wanna come in and out of work at 20 minute slots when it works for them. And we are, um, aligned in the capacity of, um, governments to allow 'em to choose that work should they wish it. But we also have seen, um, exploitation in markets where, um, genuinely vulnerable workers, um, are exposed to, um, challenges where employers who are not operating correctly can contract themselves out of, um, employment arrangements and underpaid workers.

And I do think, um, it's a really, um, fine line, the government's walking when we start looking at introducing gateway tests whilst also trying to give choice and also manage, um, exploitation. When we do it through the lens of just platform work. I think the challenge is we sometimes don't contemplate where, where, where the flow on effect might, might, might live.

Now, um, for our members, the vast bulk of contracting work tends to happen at the higher income levels. Um, they will be, um, policy specialists, they will be IT specialists for project work they might be working for. Um, they'll be engaged by a staffing firm. They'll be placed in a public service, um, role or, um, in a large corporate role to run a, you know, IT project, what have you.

Often they're quite high income earners, but they're lives and their structures, their financial structures are set around contracting 'cause would much rather contract and take their skills and move them around the marketplace. The challenge that we see with the gateway test is that the requirement that to be a contractor under that test, you have to either be able to nominate your own hours or outsource your work, um, is very challenging for that type of contracting arrangement.

Now, we do appreciate that the government's intent wasn't to make a change to the existing scenario for those types of contractors, but what we've seen historically is that once you make a test, you know, in New Zealand law as in Australian law for a long time, engaging contractors is a little bit gray. And often any determination around an employee or a, or a contractor or the classification of worker is gonna come down to a legal test.

And quite often, um, these, uh, contractors assigned, uh, agreements up front that they prefer to be a contractor. Um, and there hasn't been real a precedent there that given clarity for employers. So it's still a bit of a legal gray area, if you will. Um, the introduction of a gateway test in that scenario is that it makes that gray space a little bit more black and white.

And we've seen historically that if there is a court proceeding where someone may challenge their engagement as a contractor versus, um, employment arrangement, um, the access to a gateway test that, that, that contractor may not meet means that they're more likely to be determined as an employer could get reclassified as an employee even though that person had chosen to come in as a contractor.

Our concern, the reason we have this concern as, I guess I've seen it play out in Australia, and we've seen it around the world where a lot of the focus on the test for what is a contractor is very much focused on the emerging contractor around platform based work and things like that. But the implementation of it has massive flow on effects for, um, how people can choose to live and work in contracting arrangements in other sectors in Australia.

Um, the government moved a couple of years ago to, um, redefine, you know, what an employee was, if you like. And so in doing so, they've essentially classified a lot of those contractor based roles as employees by the nature of what they do. They go in regularly. They might have, you know, a year working on a project. They work nine to five alongside, um, you know, colleagues who are doing similar work with scheduled hours.

And in redefining it, um, the argument that's been put is that, you know, well these contractors, if they want to still be contractors, by all means they can be contractors. No one's stopping them from being contractors, but it just means there'll be a tested law down the track. The problem that we found in Australia is that the risk associated with engaging somebody as a contractor, um, under this new definition is significant because to the point made by the, um, the evidence earlier is that there's a huge financial liability you are carrying if

down the track, you know, in five years time, six years time, um, a contracting arrangement that was agreed when there was a good relationship with that person and they wanted it, things can change in five years time. If they then go back and question, um, their engagement as a contractor and, and wanna put the case that they have been always an employee.

Um, the liability for that business then is actually back that five to six years. They've gotta basically cover, you know, kiwi savers, um, annual leave and a whole raft of things that they hadn't considered, right. So in Australia, the way it's worked has been that businesses, when it much riskier to engage someone in that path, in that way, um, they've moved to put them into employment models, but along casual employment rather than contracting.

And what we've seen in Australia is a real frustration from people who want to be contractors who are earning a hundred thousand dollars a year, but no one will take them on as a contractor because the risk is just too big. So I think it's, I'm just here today, um, at the very high level to just say, look, think outside of the funnel that has been the focal point of this legislation and whether or not you've seen our submission, we've posed a few different ways that we might be able to cut it, whether it's a more enhanced, um,

contractor declaration form, whether it's going to be a way of including or expanding out the test for what a contractor is, um, around, um, adding a control mechanism so you're not just sourcing, outsourcing the work or control over your hours, but you might be able to say you have a level of influence over signing on and agreeing hours in advance.

Um, there's multiple ways to do it and we don't want to get involved in all the other myriad of issues that you are facing with this piece, but we just wanted to make sure that irrespective of what comes out, that the, the, the, the test itself and the legislation itself is very clear that, um, it's not intending to, um, it's not intending to render, um, by omission other forms of contracting as employment, which our big concern is essentially that might happen through the courts down the track. Great, Thank you very much for that.

Um, we have a question from Camilla Blich. Hi Kra. Thanks for your submission. I just had a question around the contracting points that you were making. Mm-hmm. Are you at all, um, concerned that some of these changes, uh, might lead to situations where people are not contractors being, um, classified as contractors, as we, as the government intends to move away from the true nature of employment test, Um, the contractors might be classified as not contractors?

Yes, I do think so. And so our concern is that the other Way around, sorry, that, um, that employees will be class classified as contractors. Oh, I guess there are scenarios yes, where I guess that could happen. It's, it's unusual and I think this comes down to the question that was asked earlier that I, and I flagged earlier around, or the point I raised earlier about, um, protecting genuinely vulnerable workers.

I do think that the challenge we face is when you are looking at, and you'll see from our submission, we've actually put in place a recommendation, maybe even thinking about a threshold where the contractor test the gateway test applies beneath a particular threshold. Because I do think, um, when you're looking at vulnerable workers, genuinely vulnerable workers, it's unusual for them to be able to contract out, subcontract out their work, people who wanted to be employees, but are forced into contracting arrangements,

really have that control. So I do think it strikes them up, but that vulnerable component of the sector, and I don't think it's effective in trying to, um, create protections for people who have inadvertently found themselves forced into a contracting arrangement they thought was employment. It's one of the reasons. So no, I don't think it's going to capture those people who wanted to be employed and are contract is because they really have the capacity to outsource their work or have that level of control.

But I do think, um, that's one of the reasons we suggested maybe a very strongly beefed up, um, contractor declaration test. I think one of the challenges our members have seen in the marketplace in New Zealand is that, um, and we see this across, um, construction sometimes where our members get very frustrated about creating a level playing field and seeing some, you know, um, rogue operators in market who will, um, use language barriers and other forms of disadvantage to try and contract their way out of minimum entitlement, you know,

and employment entitlement. And that frustrates us for two reasons. One, it gives the reputa, you know, the industry a really bad name, and we have a lot of very professional, obviously, peak body people who, um, our members are professional operators and proud of what they do. Um, the other reason is because it skews the playing field, right? People are undercutting in market based on not leading what would be minimum employment entitlement.

So, um, we have a shared interest in stamping that out. And I think what they shared with me was that a, a beefed up contractor, a contractors of declaration form might help if it leads with be aware if you are choosing, because all we're about is making sure people who want to be contractors can be, right.

And we've seen inadvertently in Australia that option's gone for them now that it, it's out of the, it's not even offered to them because of the risk associated. And so if we can beef it up so that people are clear on what they are giving up, right, what they're giving up as their entitlements is the first piece, and then clear upon what their, um, obligations are gonna be once they sign that piece of paper, we think that's a better deterrent. Great.

Thank you. Um, I'll have to cut you off there. Sorry, Brooke. Thank you for your time. Appreciate it. Um, it was very insightful. Thank you all. Appreciate it. Okay, team, who have we got next? Have we got the Freightways group at all?
Uh, from the, it looks like we have, um, Megan Gunderson for, um, is that Fair Pay legal?

The, the four limbs of the key things that we wanted to talk about, the first one's pretty quick, uh, written agreement that specifies a contractor relationship is, is a no brainer. We've done that forever. We argue that it should be in simple, plain English.

We also argue that contractors should have the opportunity to go and get independent advice on that from a lawyer and accountant, uh, so that everyone understands what it is that they're getting into. So we, we have no issue with that. We actually think that is really should be the primary test as to whether you're a contractor or not. The the next three, we just think have a little bit of nuance.

Um, we think there's a little bit of gray in here.

So the first one is around freedom to work for others. In the model that we adopt, what we work to do is to make sure every freightways contractor gets a, a sustainable level of income out of their runs. And the average income for a Freightways courier contract is $507 a day.

At the moment. What we do is we gear those payments around a daily rate. And so while we have no issue in principle for a contractor working for others, it has to be outside of the day in which they've contracted to us. And so typically for us, that'll be Monday to Friday. Uh, some contractors, it might be a Saturday morning.

Uh, that part's problematic. So it's just a definitional thing. The first one is either being required to work set times, or be able to freely subcontract. Reality is our customers are generally open from eight in the morning till five at night. And so we need to operate within those times. So that timing is typically set by the hours of the customers that the contractor serves. So then we come to the second part, be able to freely subcontract and our, our drivers do that. Now. Our drivers, um, will frequently and often subcontract out the run on given days, weeks, months.

Uh, if they're going overseas for a holiday, they can subcontract out that run for a month. If they want to have every Friday off, they can subcontract out the run every Friday. There's, there's a real high degree of flexibility there. But we do vet the relief driver. We do vet that substitute driver, and we do that for a range of reasons.
